summary: This lesson covers the irregular informal positive command of 'hacer' (haz), introduces the optional subjunctive use that turns 'where/how/what' into 'wherever/however/whatever', teaches 'seguir' (to follow/continue) with its e->i stem change, and introduces the irregular subjunctive of 'ser' (sea).

teachings.summary: The informal positive command of hacer is irregular: 'haz' (not 'hace'). H-a-z.
teachings.rule: Informal positive command of hacer = haz (irregular).

teachings.summary: An optional use of the subjunctive: putting the verb after donde/como/lo que into the subjunctive changes the meaning from 'where/how/what' to 'wherever/however/whatever'. This is not obligatory -- the indicative version is correct too, just with a different meaning.
teachings.rule: donde + indicative = where; donde + subjunctive = wherever. como + indicative = how; como + subjunctive = however. lo que + indicative = what; lo que + subjunctive = whatever. cuando + indicative (general) vs cuando + subjunctive = whenever.
teachings.notes: This is optional -- 'haz lo que prefieres' is perfectly correct Spanish, meaning 'do what you prefer'. The subjunctive version adds the 'ever' nuance.

teachings.summary: Seguir means to follow and also to continue/carry on. It has the same e->i stem change as elegir: sigo (I follow), sigue (he follows), but seguimos (we follow -- unstressed, no change). Related verbs: conseguir (to get/manage to), perseguir (to chase).
teachings.rule: Stem change e->i when stressed: sigo, sigues, sigue, seguimos, siguen. Same pattern in related verbs: consigo, persigo.

examples.english: I'll follow you wherever you go.

teachings.notes: Related verbs share the irregularity: conseguir (to get/manage to) -> consigo; perseguir (to chase) -> persigo.

teachings.summary: The subjunctive of ser is irregular with the base 'sea'. Forms: sea, seas, seamos, sean. The expression 'o sea' (literally 'or it might be') is a very common filler word, like 'uhh' or 'I mean'.
teachings.rule: Subjunctive of ser: sea (I/he/she/you formal), seas (you informal), seamos (we), sean (they/you plural).

examples.english: I prefer it to be like this.
Whatever will be, will be. / Whatever it is.
I mean... / In other words... (filler expression)

teachings.notes: 'O sea' is extremely common in spoken Spanish as a discourse filler.

vocabulary.english: to follow / to continue / to carry on
vocabulary.derivation: E->i stem change; related to conseguir, perseguir
Literally 'or it might be'; uses subjunctive of ser
